article_title: The 7 Proxmox Commands I Use Constantly in My Home Lab
core_thesis: While Proxmox offers a powerful GUI, mastering a handful of CLI commands is essential for efficiently managing a multi-node home lab cluster, especially when using Ceph storage. These commands provide quick insights into cluster health, storage status, VM/container states, and performance, enabling faster troubleshooting and automation.
detailed_summary: The article presents seven essential Proxmox CLI commands that the author uses daily in his multi-node home lab with Ceph storage. The commands cover cluster management (pvecm status, pvecm nodes), Ceph health (ceph -s), storage status (pvesm status), VM listing (qm list), container listing (pct list), node performance (pveperf), and VM lock removal (qm unlock). Each command is explained with typical output and practical use cases, such as checking quorum before maintenance, diagnosing slow disk performance, or unlocking a VM after a failed migration. The author emphasizes that these commands are not only for troubleshooting but also for automation in scripts and CI/CD pipelines. The article concludes by encouraging readers to learn CLI commands alongside the GUI for a more well-rounded administration skill set.

entities.name: Brandon Lee
entities.role: Author and owner of Virtualization Howto, experienced IT professional and Proxmox user.

entities.name: Proxmox VE
entities.role: Open-source virtualization platform used for managing VMs and containers.

entities.name: Ceph
entities.role: Distributed storage system used as shared storage in the author's Proxmox cluster.

key_data_points: pvecm status shows cluster quorum status, votes, and membership; essential before maintenance to ensure cluster health (🟢 relevant to your homelab: critical for multi-node cluster stability).

ceph -s provides a quick overview of Ceph cluster health, including HEALTH_OK/HEALTH_WARN/HEALTH_ERR status, OSD count, and placement group health (🟢 relevant to your homelab: vital for diagnosing storage issues in Ceph-backed environments).

pvesm status lists all storage backends (local, LVM, Ceph RBD) with total/used/available capacity and active/inactive status (🟢 relevant to your homelab: helps prevent datastore full situations and troubleshoot storage connectivity).

qm list displays VM IDs, names, status (running/stopped), memory, and boot disk size; VM IDs are required for other qm commands (🟢 relevant to your homelab: foundational for VM management and scripting).
pct list shows LXC container IDs and status; container IDs are needed for pct start/stop/exec commands (🟢 relevant to your homelab: essential for managing lightweight containers).

pveperf measures CPU BOGOMIPS, regex speed, disk buffered reads, and average seek time; seek time is key for diagnosing disk latency (🟢 relevant to your homelab: useful for benchmarking new hardware or troubleshooting slow storage).

qm unlock <vmid> removes a lock on a VM when tasks like migrations or backups get stuck; should only be used after confirming no task is still running via ps aux | grep qm (🟢 relevant to your homelab: a lifesaver for recovering hung VMs).
methodology_steps: Run 'pvecm status' to check cluster quorum and node membership before any maintenance.
Run 'pvecm nodes' for a simplified view of cluster node status.
Run 'ceph -s' to check Ceph cluster health, OSD status, and placement group health.
Run 'pvesm status' to view all storage backends, their capacity, and active status.
Run 'qm list' to list all VMs on the current node with their VMIDs and status.
Run 'pct list' to list all LXC containers on the current node with their IDs and status.
Run 'pveperf' to benchmark CPU and disk performance; focus on average seek time for disk latency.
If a VM is locked, run 'qm unlock <vmid>' after verifying no task is still running with 'ps aux | grep qm'.
conclusions: The CLI commands presented are indispensable for daily Proxmox cluster management, providing quick health checks and troubleshooting capabilities that complement the GUI.
Automation and scripting benefit greatly from these commands, as they can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines or monitoring tools to programmatically manage VMs, containers, and storage.
Knowing these commands makes a homelab administrator more self-sufficient and efficient, especially in environments with multiple nodes and shared storage like Ceph.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly applicable to your homelab and DevOps interests, providing a concise set of CLI commands for managing a Proxmox cluster with Ceph storage. As a programmer, you can integrate these commands into scripts for automation, such as programmatically checking cluster health before deployments or unlocking VMs in CI/CD pipelines. The commands also align with your Red Teaming needs by enabling rapid troubleshooting of infrastructure that might host target systems. Additionally, the article's focus on practical, daily-use commands fits your ambition to build efficient, automated homelab workflows.
extra_advice: Consider wrapping these commands into a single health-check script (e.g., a bash script that runs pvecm status, ceph -s, pvesm status, and pveperf) and outputting a summary. You can then integrate this script into your monitoring stack (e.g., Prometheus node exporter or a simple cron job) to get regular health snapshots. For Red Teaming, you could use these commands to quickly assess the state of a target Proxmox environment if you gain shell access.
